The territory of these kingdoms corresponds to a modern-day area including Yemen, and extending into Oman, north to the Arabian oasis of Dedan, to Ethiopia, and even as far along as the East African Coast, into modern Tanzania. description_short: Box, wood, cylindrical, with flat base and slightly sloping sides, 14th century, Egypt or Yemen. The exterior is overlaid with black, red, yellow, and green lacquers and carved through to form a polychrome decorative design with scrolling arabesques, quatrefoil roundels, lobed rectangular panels, and stylized thuluth calligraphy.
description_long: Box, wood, cylindrical, with flat base and slightly sloping sides and a slight recess at the upper edge to receive a lid, 14th century, Egypt or Yemen. The exterior wooden surface is overlaid in successive layers of black, red, yellow, and green lacquer and carved through to reveal a polychrome decoration. The broad central band is composed of lobed rectangular panels containing scrolling arabesques around quatrefoil roundels, with panel borders that entwine and continue into lobed concentric quatrefoils dividing each panel. Below is a band of stylized thuluth calligraphy interrupted by roundels containing quatrefoil panels. The base and interior are plain, with remains of monochrome lacquer. The object measures 16.2 cm in diameter.
description_col2: Box, wood, lacquered and carved, cylindrical with flat base, slightly sloping sides, and a recessed upper edge for a lid, attributed to the Rasulid period, 14th century, Egypt or Yemen. The exterior is overlaid with successive layers of black, red, yellow, and green lacquer, then carved through to create a polychrome decorative scheme; the broad central band has lobed rectangular panels containing scrolling arabesques around quatrefoil roundels, with panel borders that entwine and continue into lobed concentric quatrefoils dividing each panel, and a lower band of stylized thuluth calligraphy interrupted by roundels containing quatrefoil panels. The base and interior are plain, with remains of monochrome lacquer. Diameter 16.2 cm.

Rautenburghs Trompe l'œil with letters Estimate: 6,000 - 8,000 GBP Italian, Sienese, 15th century Figure of the Virgin Estimate: 5,000 - 7,000 USD Bolognese School, early 17th century Portrait of a boy, bust-length, in a white ruff Estimate: 8,000 - 12,000 GBP Charles Théodore Frère Encampment in the desert Estimate: 40,000 - 60,000 GBP Charles James Theriat The Caravan Estimate: 30,000 - 50,000 GBP Nasreddine (Etienne) Dinet A Captive Audience Estimate: 50,000 - 70,000 GBP Originally from the storied collection of Christiane and Antonin Besse who lovingly built a collection of South Arabian sculpture while living in the then British colony of Aden in the middle 20 th century (see ), this sculpture stands as a compelling testament to the religious life, social structure, and artistic sophistication of ancient South Arabia, offering rare insight into a culture whose visual traditions remain less familiar than those of its contemporaries, yet equally rich and expressive. This lot has been withdrawn Lot Details Description A South Arabian Alabaster Figure of a Woman, Qataban 3rd Century B.C./1st Century A.D. Origins II Origins II A South Arabian Alabaster Figure of a Woman, Qataban, 3rd Century B.C./1st Century A.D. Carved from translucent calcite-alabaster, a material prized for its luminous qualities, the figure displays the stylized aesthetic characteristic of South Arabian figurative art: a frontal pose, simplified bodily forms, and an emphasis on facial presence and symmetry. Originally, such figures may have featured painted details or inlaid facial detailing, such as extant here in the figures recessed eyes, enhancing their lifelike presence and ritual impact. description_long: Coin, silver. Ancient Greek posthumous tetradrachm of Alexander the Great, attributed to the Hellenistic period and associated with the Near East (Western Asiatic) and Southern Europe. The tetradrachm was Alexander’s silver stater, or standard denomination, and was minted in greater numbers than any other Alexander denomination. The obverse depicts Alexander wearing the Nemean lion-skin headdress, one of Herakles’ typical attributes, and the reverse shows the Greek god Zeus seated to the left on a throne, holding an eagle in his right hand and a sceptre in his left. The tetradrachm was Alexander’s standard silver denomination and was used as an international imperial coin in state transactions.

The object has a large truncated conical body surmounted by a flat drip tray, a tall ridged cylindrical neck, and a tapered ridged candle holder. The base of the body is engraved with a thuluth inscription recording the endowment of the candlestick by Usta Ibrahim, described as the head cauldron maker and servant of Sultan Suleyman Khan, to the "tomb of His Holiness the Sultan of Prophets." The text identifies the object as an Ottoman courtly metalwork piece associated with Suleyman the Magnificent.

text_for_matching_web: The Barakat Collection Open a larger version of the following image in a popup: Open a larger version of the following image in a popup: Open a larger version of the following image in a popup: Open a larger version of the following image in a popup: Sabean Alabaster Relief of a Horned Bull , 200 BCE - 100 CE Alabaster 10.2 x 14 cm 4 1/8 x 5 1/2 in PF.0083 Enquire %3Cdiv%20class%3D%22title_and_year%22%3E%3Cspan%20class%3D%22title_and_year_title%22%3ESabean%20Alabaster%20Relief%20of%20a%20Horned%20Bull%3C/span%3E%2C%20%3Cspan%20class%3D%22title_and_year_year%22%3E200%20BCE%20%20-%20%20100%20CE%3C/span%3E%3C/div%3E%3Cdiv%20class%3D%22medium%22%3EAlabaster%3C/div%3E%3Cdiv%20class%3D%22dimensions%22%3E10.2%20x%2014%20cm%3Cbr/%3E%0A4%201/8%20x%205%201/2%20in%3C/div%3E Further images (View a larger image of thumbnail 1 ) (View a larger image of thumbnail 2 ) (View a larger image of thumbnail 3 ) (View a larger image of thumbnail 4 ) The ancient kingdom of Saba ruled over the lands of southern Arabia, centered in modern day Yemen. In the 1st Century A.D., the Ptolemaic Greeks discovered a sea route from India directly to the port of Alexandria, eliminating Saba from this lucrative trade and ushering in the decline of Sabean prosperity. Carved from one smooth block of alabaster, this beautiful high relief sculpture signifies an important period of art in the South Arabian region, using bold curves and delicate decoration. Although gold and silver deposits were present, the chief source of their vast wealth was derived from their veritable monopoly of two of the most coveted materials in ancient times: frankincense and myrrh, resinous gums obtained from certain trees that only grow in Southern Arabia and were literally worth their weight in gold. Perhaps their greatest accomplishment was the Great Dam of Marib, a monumental construction that brought water from the mountains both to the city and to the crop fields. The dam was in continual use (accounting for timely repairs) until the 6th Century A.D. Saba is perhaps better known as Sheba, the Hebrew word for the kingdom, whose famous Queen was recounted as having visited Solomon in the pages of the Old Testament. Ruins of fortresses and walled towns are evident and remnants of their extensive irrigation system that turned the desert into a paradise still cover the land.

text_for_matching_web: Standing male figure ca. late 1st millennium BCE Not on view This standing male figure is a funerary or votive object intended to be placed in a tomb or temple. Dressed in a long, plain garment reaching almost to the ankles, the figure stands in a fully frontal pose. The base, which is carved in one piece with the figure, bears the name of the individual and his clan—Sadiqim Ma'ad. The eyes and eyebrows are carved to hold inlays, now missing, and the arms are bent at the elbows, extending out at right angles to the body. One hand is clenched in a fist, pierced vertically to hold some object, perhaps a scepter, now lost. The other hand is open. The undersurface of the base and the top and back of the figure's head are roughly carved. Such statues were often set in niches, and the surfaces not visible were consequently left unfinished on many examples. The placement of the ears high on the head, the aquiline nose, the small mouth, and the fully frontal pose are all characteristic of human images made in this region. description_long: Standing male figure, alabaster (calcite), ca. late 1st millennium BCE. This funerary or votive object was intended for placement in a tomb or temple. The figure stands frontally and wears a long, plain garment reaching almost to the ankles. It is carved in one piece with a base that bears the name of the individual and his clan, Sadiqim Ma'ad. The eyes and eyebrows are carved to receive inlays, now missing. The arms are bent at the elbows and extend outward at right angles to the body; one hand is clenched in a fist and pierced vertically to hold an object, perhaps a scepter, now lost, while the other hand is open. The undersurface of the base and the top and back of the head are roughly carved, consistent with statues that were set in niches and left unfinished on non-visible surfaces. The ears are placed high on the head, and the aquiline nose, small mouth, and fully frontal pose are characteristic of human images made in southwestern Arabia.

description_short: Bronze votive statue of a man, South Arabian, 6th century B.C. The standing figure has the right arm bent, the left arm close to the body, and clenched fists pierced through. It wears a pleated loincloth fitted over the hips to the knees, crossed and open at the front, and held by a belt.
description_long: Bronze votive statue of a man, South Arabian, 6th century B.C. This ex-voto sculpture represents a standing male figure with the right arm bent and the left arm held close to the body; the fists are clenched and pierced. The figure is dressed in a pleated loincloth moulded to the hips and extending to the knees, crossed and open at the front, and secured by a belt. The dating given in the source is supported by the stylistic treatment of the garment, whose drapery recalls the Achaemenid friezes of Persepolis, and by the hairstyle, described as volute-like curls comparable to those of Assyrians, Persians, and Greeks of the 6th century B.C.
description_col2: Bronze masculine votive statue, South Arabia, 6th century B.C., representing a standing man with the right arm bent and the left arm close to the body, both fists closed and pierced; he wears a pleated loincloth fitted to the hips and extending to the knees, crossed and open at the front, held in place by a belt. The stylistic treatment of the drapery is compared to Achaemenid friezes at Persepolis, and the hairstyle to the volute curls of Assyrians, Persians, and Greeks of the 6th century B.C. The figure is described as an ex-voto made in the likeness of the worshipper, with parallels in three other bronze sculptures in the Sanaa Museum.

text_for_matching_web: Incense burner ca. mid-1st millennium BCE Not on view This bronze incense burner from southwestern Arabia consists of a cylindrical cup set on a conical base. A rectangular architectural form suggesting a facade extends upward from the cup’s front. Its face is decorated with two serpents flanking a round disk set within a crescent, all in low relief. Cast separately, an ibex standing on a plinth projects from the front of the incense burner and may serve as a handle. From the middle of the first millennium B.C. until the sixth century A.D., the kingdoms of southwestern Arabia gained considerable wealth and power through their control of the trade in incense between Arabia and the lands of the Mediterranean seacoast. Frankincense and myrrh, gum resins that are native to southern Arabia, were widely valued in the ancient world for the preparation of incense, perfumes, cosmetics, and medicines, as well as for use in religious and funerary ceremonies. The importance of incense in the religion of southwestern Arabia is reflected in this object

description_long: Incense burner, bronze, from southwestern Arabia, dated to ca. mid-1st millennium BCE. The vessel consists of a cylindrical cup set on a conical base, with a rectangular architectural form rising from the front of the cup and suggesting a facade. The facade is decorated in low relief with two serpents flanking a round disk set within a crescent. A separately cast ibex standing on a plinth projects from the front of the burner and may have functioned as a handle. The object is associated with the importance of incense in the religion of southwestern Arabia.

text_for_matching_web: Standing bull ca. mid- to late 1st millennium BCE Not on view This bronze bull stands on a double-based plinth. Its head is strongly sculpted with upright ears behind horns that curve up and slightly inward. Heavy brows arch over the eyes, which are inlaid with shell. The tail hangs straight down to the hooves and parallels those of southwestern Arabian statues of standing bulls sculpted in alabaster, suggesting that this piece dates to the late first millennium B.C. like the alabaster bulls. By the middle of the first millennium B.C., kingdoms had emerged in southwestern Arabia whose power was based on control of the trade in frankincense and myrrh, which are native to the region. The kingdoms' immense wealth is reflected in the use of bronze to cast large sculptures, as well as smaller objects, which were produced throughout most of the first millennium B.C. Bulls are commonly represented and can be found on funerary stelae, seals, and sculpture. The wealth of the kingdom is legendary, and is primarily attributable to Saba’s position at the crossroads of the ancient world, receiving consignments (particularly of incense) from all across the Middle East, Asia and the Mediterranean basin. Technically, the Sabeans were one of four major powers in this area, also including the Minaeans, the Qatabanians and the Hadramites, but the peoples as a whole became subsumed as a single entity by the middle of the first millennium BC. This piece depicts a feminine head and neck, and appears to have once served as a grave marker, or perhaps as part of a larger alabaster structure. Their religion and mythology fuelled the themes of their sculptural works – primarily anthropomorphic and zoomorphic statuary – while their contact with other cultures and nations led to a highly derived and distinctive style.

description_short: Cartonnage mummy mask, polychrome painted and gilded, Egyptian. Depicted standing, with both arms bent projecting forward, the left hand closed in a fist, wearing a short plain tunic; the face has almond-shaped eyes and straight eyebrows, both recessed for now-missing inlays, and the beard is indicated by a line starting from the ears and running below the chin.
description_long: Cartonnage mummy mask, Egyptian, made of polychrome painted cartonnage with gilding. The mask is depicted standing, with both arms bent and projecting forward, the left hand closed in a fist. It wears a short plain tunic. The face is modelled with almond-shaped eyes and straight eyebrows, both recessed for now-missing inlays. The beard is indicated by a line starting from the ears and running below the chin. The object measures 15 in (38 cm) high.
description_col2: Object type: cartonnage mummy mask, material: painted cartonnage, Egyptian, polychrome painted and gilded, depicting a standing figure with both arms bent and projecting forward, the left hand closed in a fist, wearing a short plain tunic; the face has almond-shaped eyes and straight eyebrows, both recessed for now-missing inlays, and a beard indicated by a line running from the ears below the chin; height 15 in. (38 cm).

description_short: Stele, limestone. South Arabian limestone stele with an angular human head set on a rectangular plaque, inscribed in Sabaean, with a defined brow ridge, sharp nose, diamond-shaped eyes with inner irises, a slit mouth, closely cropped beard, and ears in relief at the sides; some red slip remains. Height 14½ in (36.7 cm).
description_long: Stele, limestone. South Arabian limestone stele, circa 3rd-1st century B.C., formed as an angular human head set on a rectangular plaque. The face is inscribed in Sabaean and has a defined brow ridge, sharp nose, diamond-shaped eyes with inner irises, a slit for the mouth, and a closely cropped beard. The ears are rendered in relief at the sides, and some red slip remains on the surface. Height 14½ in (36.7 cm). Provenance: English private collection.
description_col2: South Arabian limestone stele, carved as an angular human head set on a rectangular plaque and inscribed in Sabaean; the face has a defined brow ridge, sharp nose, diamond-shaped eyes with inner irises, a slit mouth, closely cropped beard, and ears in relief at the sides, with some red slip remaining; height 14½ in. (36.7 cm); circa 3rd–1st century B.C.

description_short: Female votive figure, marble with gesso-painted surface, standing frontally on a small rectangular integral plinth and dated to circa 1st century B.C. The figure wears a long dress and holds a rectangular hollowed-out votive bowl.
description_long: Female votive figure, marble with an applied gesso layer painted with coloured details, standing frontally on a small rectangular integral plinth and dated to circa 1st century B.C. The figure wears a long dress, waisted and ending at the calves above ankle bracelets, with incised linear and cross-hatched ornament over the legs and around the upper arms. She is shown with strands of necklaces composed of triangular pendants and a large central crescentic pendant above the breasts, and her arms are bent at the elbows with the forearms projecting forward to hold a rectangular hollowed-out votive bowl with incised panels. The oval face has a pointed chin, strong browline, incised arched brows, large eyes with drilled pupils, a straight nose, and a small mouth. The hair flares below the ears and falls to the shoulders, with incised linear banded details and a diadem of circular pendants above the forehead. Height: 31 in (79 cm).
description_col2: Female votive figure, marble with gesso and painted decoration, standing frontally on a small rectangular integral plinth and wearing a long waisted dress falling to the calves above ankle bracelets; the surface is covered with an applied layer of gesso with coloured details incised, including linear and cross-hatched bands on the dress and banded details in the hair. The figure wears necklaces composed of triangular pendants with a large central crescentic pendant above the breasts, and a diadem of circular pendants above the forehead. The arms are bent at the elbows with the forearms projecting forward to hold a rectangular hollowed-out votive bowl with incised panels. The oval face has a pointed chin, strong browline, incised arched brows, large eyes with drilled pupils, a straight nose, and a small mouth; the hair flares below the ears and falls to the shoulders. South Arabian, circa 1st century B.C.; height 31 in. (79 cm).
